Case_Title: State VS Abdur Rahim
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Sections 498 & 517
Ratio_Decidendi: An application for disposal of seized articles can be filed under section 517 of the Code before the proper Court after conclusion of trial. The High Court Division acted illegally and without proper jurisdiction in releasing the seized goods at the time of issuance of Rule in an application under section 498 of the Code.
Judgment_Content: MM Ruhul Amin J: This appeal by leave is directed against the judgment and orders dated 9-12-1997 and 21-5-1998 passed by a Division Bench of the High Court Division in Criminal Miscellaneous Case No.4692 of 1997. The Miscellaneous Case was registered upon an application filed under section 498 (wrongly written as 496) of the Code of Criminal Procedure. The High Court Division issued Rule to show cause as to ad interim bail was also granted to the respondent why the respondent shall not be enlarged on bail and and the Collector (Commissioner) of Customs, Zia International Airport was directed to give delivery payment of usual customs duty as per rules within one week from the date of receipt of the copy of the order. By order dated 21-5-1998 the High Court deliver 320 tolas of gold to the respondent seized Division again directed the Customs Authority to from him on payment of customs duty as per relevant Rules within one week from the date of receipt of the sent to appropriate Bench for issuing Rule upon them copy of the order, failing which the matter would be for committing contempt of Court

Case_Title: State VS Abdur Rahim
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Sections 498 & 517
Ratio_Decidendi: An application for disposal of seized articles can be filed under section 517 of the Code before the proper Court after conclusion of trial. The High Court Division acted illegally and without proper jurisdiction in releasing the seized goods at the time of issuance of Rule in an application under section 498 of the Code.
Judgment_Content: 2. Short facts are that the respondent, Abdur Rahim, went to Bangkok and Singapore. While returning he carried with him 320 tolas of gold, a micro oven, 30 computer processors and other personal affects allowable under Schedule 2 of Rule 7 of the Baggage Rules. He was detained by a Customs Officer due to an altercation between him and the Officer and he was implicated in Canton ment PS Case No.12(11)/97 on a first information report lodged by Customs Inspector on the allegations, inter alia, that the respondent landed at Zia International Airport from Thai Airways on 10-11-1997 at 12-25 hours. After completing all formalities he came to the customs counter where he gave a declaration regarding micro oven only without giving any declaration of gold bars and computer processors and while he was moving towards the Red Channel he was challenged by the Customs Surveillance Team and was taken to the room of the Officer-in-Charge and on search 320 tolas of gold from two bags and 30 computer processors from his briefcase were recovered. Those articles were seized and a seizure list was prepared and the respondent was sent to the police station for prosecution under sections 2(3), 16 and 32 of the Customs Act, 1969, under section 8(1) of the Foreign Exchange Regulations Act, 1947 and under section 3(1) of the Imports and Exports (Control) Act, 1960 read with section 25B of the Special Powers Act, 1974. The respondent's defence was that he wanted to pay the duties on the articles after voluntarily opening the briefcase and the bags but the customs officers on duty demanded one-fourth of the gold and as he did not agree he was taken to the room of the Customs Inspector who obtained from him a written false statement that he would be released and the articles seized would be returned on payment of duties on assessment

Case_Title: State VS Abdur Rahim
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Sections 498 & 517
Ratio_Decidendi: An application for disposal of seized articles can be filed under section 517 of the Code before the proper Court after conclusion of trial. The High Court Division acted illegally and without proper jurisdiction in releasing the seized goods at the time of issuance of Rule in an application under section 498 of the Code.
Judgment_Content: 3.The respondent moved bail petition before the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, Dhaka. The preferred Criminal Miscellaneous Case No.5229 of application for hail was rejected and the respondens 1997 before the Court of Session, Dhaka and the learned Additional Sessions Judge, 7th Coun the prayer for hail. Against the said order of Dhaka by his order dated 26-11-1997 also rejected rejection of bail, the respondent filed an application under section 498 (wrongly written as under section 496) of the Code of Criminal Procedure before the High Court Division. The High Court Division while issuing Rule passed the impugned order 4. Leave was granted to consider the submission that the High Court Division in exercise of its jurisdiction under section 498 of the Code of Criminal Procedure has transgressed its limiti dealing with the goods seized, and, as such, the impugned orders to release the seized goods are without lawful authority and the submission that the seized goods, subject matter of the offences alleged, cannot be ordered to be released till the conclusion of the proceeding nor such orders can be passede parte and the further submission that the High Court Division ought not to have given full relief to the respondent including release of gold in an application for bail under section 498 of the Code of Criminal Procedure as the same does not authorise any court, at the stage of the case at the material time, to pass an order for release of seized articles

Case_Title: State VS Abdur Rahim
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Sections 498 & 517
Ratio_Decidendi: An application for disposal of seized articles can be filed under section 517 of the Code before the proper Court after conclusion of trial. The High Court Division acted illegally and without proper jurisdiction in releasing the seized goods at the time of issuance of Rule in an application under section 498 of the Code.
Judgment_Content: 5. We have heard Mr Faisal H Khan, the learned Assistant Attorney-General for the appellant and Mr Md Nawab Ali, the learned Advocate on-Record for the respondent and perused the impugned orders of the High Court Division and other connected papers 6. The learned Assistant Attorney-General submitted that 320 tolas of gold are subject matter of offences alleged to have been committed by the respondent and if the seized goods are released on an application under section 498 of the Code of Criminal Procedure before trial, there remains nothing to be tried by the trial Court. He then submitted that in an appropriate case an application under section 517 of the Code of Criminal Procedure may be filed before the proper Court for release of the seized articles on conclusion of trial of the case but not before the High Court Division and it is not within the scope of an application under section 498 of the Code of Criminal Procedure to pass such an order for release of the seized articles 7. Mr Md Nawab Ali, learned Advocate-on-Record for the respondent found it difficult to support the impugned orders of the High Court Division

Case_Title: State VS Abdur Rahim
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Sections 498 & 517
Ratio_Decidendi: An application for disposal of seized articles can be filed under section 517 of the Code before the proper Court after conclusion of trial. The High Court Division acted illegally and without proper jurisdiction in releasing the seized goods at the time of issuance of Rule in an application under section 498 of the Code.
Judgment_Content: 8. Section 498 of the Code of Criminal Procedure is within Chapter XIX of the Code dealing with bail matters. Section 498 of the Code of Criminal Procedure deals with the power of the High Court Division or the Court of Session to direct admission to bail of any person in any case 9. Therefore, we fail to understand how the High Court Division could pass the impugned order for release of the seized articles to the respondent at the time of issuance of Rule in an application for bail filed under section 498(wrongly written as 496) of the Code of Criminal Procedure 10. Chapter XLIII of the Code of Criminal Procedure deals with the disposal of property regarding which any offence has been committed. Thus it is clear that an application for disposal of seized articles can be filed under section 517 of the Code of Criminal Procedure before the proper Court after conclusion of trial

Case_Title: Bangladesh, represented by the Secretary, Ministry of Works, Housing and Settlement and others VS Md Abu Bakar and others
Subject_Law: Constitution of Bangladesh, 1972 - Articles 102 & 103
Ratio_Decidendi: The High Court Division directed the respondent Nos. 1-3 to act in accordance with law and no direction was given to them for allotment of a plot to the writ petitioner. So, there is no cogent reason to interfere with the decision of the High Court Division.
Judgment_Content: Md Tafazzul Islam J: This petition for leave to appeal at the instance of the writ respondents is directed against the judgment and order dated 9-2-2002 passed by the High Court Division in Writ Petition No. 673 of 2001 2. The respondent No. 1 filed the above writ petition stating, inter alia, that he revas perally entitled to get allotment of a plot as affected person because the land of his father was acquired and he and other heirs already received compensation from the authority and that he made a formal prayer for allotment of the land as described in his application but no plot was allotted to him and that the last intimation that was given to him is on 1-2-2002 and after that the petitioner was not informed anything as to the fate of his prayer. Then the High Court Division issued Rule calling upon the respondent Nos. 1-3 to show cause as to why they shall not be directed to act in accordance with law for granting allotment of a plot to the petitioner as an effected person. The Rule was opposed on behalf of the respondents submitting that all the plots were already allotted to other persons long ago and there was no scope for allotment of any plot to the petitioner and further that there is also no excess land for allotment to the petitioner. The High Court Division, after hearing, made the Rule absolute 3. The learned Counsel appearing for the petitioner submits that the High Court Division erred in law in making the Rule absolute inasmuch as the plot claimed by the writ petitioner having being allotted to other persons long ago there remained nothing to be allotted to the writ petitioner and further there being no proof that there is any excess land which can be allotted to the writ petitioner the High Court Division erred in law in making the Rule absolute and lastly, the writ petitioner having acquired no legal right, the writ petition is not at all maintainable

Case_Title: Bangladesh, represented by the Secretary, Ministry of Works, Housing and Settlement and others VS Md Abu Bakar and others
Subject_Law: Constitution of Bangladesh, 1972 - Articles 102 & 103
Ratio_Decidendi: The High Court Division directed the respondent Nos. 1-3 to act in accordance with law and no direction was given to them for allotment of a plot to the writ petitioner. So, there is no cogent reason to interfere with the decision of the High Court Division.
Judgment_Content: 4. As it appears the High Court Division directed the respondent Nos. 1-3 to act "in. accordance with law" and no direction was given to them for allotment of a plot to the writ petitioner 5. So, there is no cogent reason to interfere with the decision of the High Court Division The petition is dismissed

Case_Title: Secretary, Ministry of Food, Government of Bangladesh and others VS Md Sirajuddin Ahmed and others
Subject_Law: Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Section 80
Ratio_Decidendi: There is nothing in the plaint to indicate that any notice as required under section 80 of the Code was issued. It appears mandatory period of three months has not been allowed to the appellant to submit written statement. In this view there is substance in the submissions made by the Additional Attorney-General and on this score alone the suit is required to be sent back to the trial Court on remand, directing it to give reasonable time to the defendant to submit written statement and the trial Court to proceed in accordance with law.
Judgment_Content: Amirul Kabir Chowdhury J: This appeal has been preferred by the defendants-the Secretary. Ministry of Food and others against the judgment and decree passed by a Single Bench of the High Court Division in Civil Revision No. 2170 of 1998 on 7-12-1998 discharging the Rule thereby affirming the judgment and decree dated 16-11-1997 in Title Appeal No. 25 of 1997 passed by the learned Additional District Judge, 2nd Court, Narayanganj who allowed the appeal in part reversing those passed by the learned Assistant Judge, Bandar, Narayanganj dated 20-2-1997 in Title Suit No. 4 of 1996 deereeing the suit 2. The plaintiff respondent No. 1 instituted the aforesaid suit for declaration of his title and interest in the suit land stating, inter alia, that the suit property belonged to Messrs Kaknorak Company which used to deal in jute in the matter of construction of Jute Godown and excavation of canal; that subsequently, the said company became defunct and the then Government of East Pakistan, filed three certificate cases Nos. 3 of 60-61, 9 of 60-61 and 12 of 60-61 against the said company for realisation of arrear rent by putting the suit property in auction; that the plaintiff-respondent being the highest bidder purchased the suit property, the sale was confirmed on 28-7-1970 and thereafter, the said plaintiff-respondent after purchase made some improvement in the suit land measuring 16.36 acres; and he also got his name mutated on 27-9-1995 initiating Mutation Case No. 270 of 1995-1996 and paid rent up to 1995. But during the Revisional Settlement Operation the said land was found to be wrongly recorded in the name of the Government

Judgment_Content: 3. The suit was contested by the Government of Bangladesh represented by the Deputy Commis sioner, Narayanganj, Additional Deputy Commis-sioner Revenue, Narayanganj and Assistant Commissioner (Land), Bandar, Narayanganj-defendant Nos. 1-3 by filing written statement con-tending, inter alia, that the Government acquired 12.57 acres of land in land acquisition Case No. 32 of 1956-57 and 60 of 1958-59 out of the aforesaid total land for the purpose of Central Storage Depot of the food department and the same was correctly recorded in the name of the Government, that is, in the name of Food Department 4. During pendency of the suit the plaintiff-respondent amended the plaint and petitioner No. 1 was made party and order was passed directing to issue summons upon the said petitioner and others and accordingly, summonses were received by the District Controller of Food. The Manager, Central Storage Department by its memo dated 16-3-1997 informed the Government Pleader, Narayanganj, that the entire suit land measuring 19.60 acres including suit plots have been under the possession and enjoyment of the Food Department since 1956 and the same was already acquired 5. Upon hearing the parties, the trial Court decreed the suit on 20-2-1997 in favour of the plaintiff-respondents to the extent of 3.79 acres of land. The Government being aggrieved preferred an appeal and the plaintiff-respondent also filed cross objection. The Appellate Court dismissed the appeal by decreeing the suit for more area of land being 4.17 acres of land. Against the aforesaid judgment of the lower Appellate Court the leave petitioners moved the High Court Division and the Rule obtained in the Revision was discharged. Hence is this appeal

He further submits that there being of Civil Procedure the defendant-appellant has been seriously prejudiced being deprived of the chance of contesting the suit, legitimate period allowed under the law being not given to the defendant and as a result of which the defendant could not file written statement and, therefore, ex parte decree had been passed against the defendant to the serious peril of the Interests of the appellant

He further submits that the lower appellate Court considered the quantum of land purchased by the plaintiff from individual plots and also the quantum of land acquired from the individual plots and found that the plaintiffs succeeded to prove his claim of 4.17 acres of land and, therefore, decreed the same accordingly, in his favour 9. Mr Bhuiyan, lastly, submitted that though the trial Court did not allow two months' time to the added defendant No. 4, that is the appellant, to submit written statement but the added defendant-appellant preferred appeal before the District Judge and did not take advantage of the opportunity available to him for submitting the case of the appellant and produce documents in support of the claim at least as additional evidence under Order XLI, rule 27 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Judgment_Content: Provided that in a suit instituted without such notice, the Court shall allow not less than three months to the Government to submit its written statement." 12. From the aforesaid provision of law it appears that if a suit is instituted without delivering of leaving notice as provided in the law the Court shall allow not less than three months' time to the Government to submit its written statement 13. We have already noticed that the appellant I was impleaded as defendant on 24-11-1996 and the suit itself has been decreed ex parte against the appellant 1 on 20-2-1997. There is nothing in the plaint to indicate that any notice as required under section 80 of the Code of Civil Procedure were issued/served. It thus appears that mandatory period of three months has not been allowed to the appellant to enable it to submit its written statement. In this view of the matter, we find substance in the submissions made by the learned Additional Attorney-General and on this score alone the suit, according to us, is required to be sent back to the Inal Court on remand, directing the trial Court to give reasonable time to defendant No. 4 to submit written statement and the trial Court shall proceed in accordance with law

Case_Title: Secretary, Ministry of Food, Government of Bangladesh and others VS Md Sirajuddin Ahmed and others
Subject_Law: Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Section 80
Ratio_Decidendi: There is nothing in the plaint to indicate that any notice as required under section 80 of the Code was issued. It appears mandatory period of three months has not been allowed to the appellant to submit written statement. In this view there is substance in the submissions made by the Additional Attorney-General and on this score alone the suit is required to be sent back to the trial Court on remand, directing it to give reasonable time to the defendant to submit written statement and the trial Court to proceed in accordance with law.
Judgment_Content: 14. There is, however, other side of the coin. On perusal of the written statement and evidence of DW I produced on behalf of the defendant Nos. 1-3 that is the Government of Bangladesh represented by Deputy Commissioner, Narayanganj and two other officers i.e. Additional Deputy Commissioner, Revenue, Narayanganj and Assistant Commissioner Narayanganj, it appears to us that a half-hearted written statement has been filed in the The persistent case of the plaintiff-respondent is that negligent in the conduct of the case on their behalt. the plaintiff owned and possessed the suit property which was beyond the land acquired by the Government and the aforesaid defendant Nos. 1-3 were not diligent in taking proper steps to assist the Court in proper disposal of the suit. The appellants though filed appeal before the lower appellate Court also have not diligently pursued the matter. After giving our anxious consideration to the facts and circumstances, we are led to the opinion that for the laches and negligence as aforesaid the appellants should be saddled with cost In the result, the appeal is allowed. The judgment and order of the High Court Division, and the judgment and decree passed by the Additional District Judge and the Assistant Judge are hereby set aside. Title Suit No. 4 of 1996 is sent back on remand to the Court of learned Assistant Judge, Bandar, Narayanganj for disposal as directed above. The appellant No. 1, Secretary, Ministry of Food, Government of People's Republic of Bangladesh do pay cost of Taka 3,000 in favour of the plaintiff-respondent No. 1 within two months from the date of receipt of this order by the trial Court, in default, this appeal shall stand dismissed …

Case_Title: Abdul Kader VS Secretary, Election Commission and others
Subject_Law: Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance, 1983 - Section 7(2)(g)
Ratio_Decidendi: The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section. Had it been the case that rescheduling would save a loanee from being a defaulter, the legislature would have made a provision for the same in the section itself. The Court is to interpret the law as it stands, giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning.
Judgment_Content: Md Fazlul Karim J: The petitioner seeks leave to appeal against the judgment and order dated 25-7-2005 passed by the High Court Division discharging the Rule issued against declaring the petitioner a defaulter illegally cancelling his nomination paper by the respondent No. 2 and further, declaring his election as Chairman of Kuti Union Parishad void 2. The petitioner filed the writ petition stating, inter alia, that he was a former Chairman of Kuti Union Parishad and he, with a view to D contesting the office of the Chairman in the election, filed nomination paper on 1-1-2003. The respon-dent No. 3, Returning Officer, in consideration of an objection submitted by one Harunur Rashid Chowdhury of village Leashiara of Ward No. 5 of the concerned union, found that the petitioner was a loan-defaulter with Agrani Bank, Kasba Branch and rejected his nomination paper on the ground of his being disqualified under section 7(2)(g) of the Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinances, 1983

Case_Title: Abdul Kader VS Secretary, Election Commission and others
Subject_Law: Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance, 1983 - Section 7(2)(g)
Ratio_Decidendi: The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section. Had it been the case that rescheduling would save a loanee from being a defaulter, the legislature would have made a provision for the same in the section itself. The Court is to interpret the law as it stands, giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning.
Judgment_Content: 3. The petitioner being aggrieved by the said order of rejection of the Returning Officer preferred an appeal before the respondent No. 2. Th Nirbahi Officer, within time and the Thana Nirhu Officer as an appellate authority by his order datat 5-1-2003 affirmed the order dated 1-1-2003 of the Returning Officer holding him disqualified I loan-defaulter with the said Bank 4. Upon a split judgment the matter w referred to the learned third Judge who was pleased to discharge the Rule. The petitioner, being aggrieved, moved this Court 5. Mr Anisul Huq, the learned Advoca appearing for the petitioner, submitted that the respondent Nos. 2 and 3 illegally rejected the nomination paper of the petitioner as the petitione ceased to be a defaulter on the date when dows payment was accepted and the bank had assured time for six months more to repay his loan. The leamed Advocate further submitted that the Agrani Bank having rescheduled his loan with effect from 18-11 2002 the petitioner ceased to be a defaulter on 18-12 2002 and his nomination paper has been unlawfully cancelled. The learned Advocate lastly submitted tha both the Returning Officer and the Thana Nirbah Officer, Kasba erred in interpreting section 7(2)(g) of the Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance, 1983, and thereby acted illegally in cancelling the nomination paper of the petitioner

Case_Title: Abdul Kader VS Secretary, Election Commission and others
Subject_Law: Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance, 1983 - Section 7(2)(g)
Ratio_Decidendi: The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section. Had it been the case that rescheduling would save a loanee from being a defaulter, the legislature would have made a provision for the same in the section itself. The Court is to interpret the law as it stands, giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning.
Judgment_Content: 6. Section 7(2)(g) of the Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance reads as under: Section 7. Qualifications and disqualifications of Chairman and Members of Union Parishads- (2) A person shall be disqualified for election as, or for being, a Chairman of Member if-

Case_Title: Abdul Kader VS Secretary, Election Commission and others
Subject_Law: Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance, 1983 - Section 7(2)(g)
Ratio_Decidendi: The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section. Had it been the case that rescheduling would save a loanee from being a defaulter, the legislature would have made a provision for the same in the section itself. The Court is to interpret the law as it stands, giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning.
Judgment_Content: (g) he has defaulted in repaying any loan taken by him from any specified bank within the time allowed by the bank therefore 6. From the above, it is clear that on the date of submission of nomination paper the petitioner was admittedly a defaulter as contemplated by law. The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section and the legislature has not contemplated the said reschedu-ling so as to save the said admitted defaulter from the mischief of being defaulter. Had it been the case, that the rescheduling would save a loanee of being a defaulter the legislature would have made the provision for the same in the section itself. The section does not provide for any exception by any proviso thereto in the section. We are to interpret the law as it stands giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning 7. In the case of Nur Mohammad vs Bodruddoza and another reported in 42 DLR (AD) 116 it has been held that:

Case_Title: Abdul Kader VS Secretary, Election Commission and others
Subject_Law: Local Government (Union Parishads) Ordinance, 1983 - Section 7(2)(g)
Ratio_Decidendi: The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section. Had it been the case that rescheduling would save a loanee from being a defaulter, the legislature would have made a provision for the same in the section itself. The Court is to interpret the law as it stands, giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning.
Judgment_Content: "Such default in the payment of any loan has been made a disqualification to be elected or to be nominated to the election of a Chairman of Union Parishad. The reason is that the legislature wanted to eliminate such person from holding a public office because such person, who was a defaulter would not be considered suitable for performing the public duty. This was considered in two decisions of this Court in 41 DLR (AD) 68 and 40 DLR (AD) 10. It was further elaborated in Sheikh Abdus Sahur vs Returning Officer 41 DLR (AD) 30. It was observed; "The main object of the 'disqualification' provision appears to be the furtherance of economic and financial interest of the State and though it has not been expressly stated in the statute it is clear from the nature of duties and thesponsibilities of the persons constituting local bodies. It is a common knowledge that for non-payment of loans taken from State-owned banks, the national economy has been badly affected. One of the functions of Union Parishad is to help collection of Government dues, rents and taxes. Besides, members of the Union Parishad are directly involved in financial transaction in the course of their official duties and running the affairs of the Union Parishad. The fact that these persons are financially handicapped by being 'defaulters" will embarrass them in the discharge of their duties." Advocate for the petitioner. 8. In view of the above, we do not find any substance in the submissions of the learned

Ratio_Decidendi: The question of rescheduling of loan of a defaulter by the Bank did not find place in the section. Had it been the case that rescheduling would save a loanee from being a defaulter, the legislature would have made a provision for the same in the section itself. The Court is to interpret the law as it stands, giving the section its natural and ordinary meaning unless it admits of any other meaning.
Judgment_Content: The petition is dismissed

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: Amirul Kabir Chowdhury J: Yogashwar Gope, the convict petitioner, seeks leave to appeal against the judgment and order dated 12-1-2003 passed by the High Court Division in Death Reference No. 46 of 2000 rejecting the said reference and dismissing Criminal Appeal No. 2001 of 2000 with modifica tion of the sentence of death to imprisonment for life 2. The facts, in short, are that, Susanta Gope, son of Jayanta Gope, was sleeping in his house on the 8th Baishak 1404 at about 3-00 AM while the accused petitioner along with other accused went to his house, and then the petitioner and others called him and then the inmates viz Susanta (since deceased), his father Jayanta Gope, mother Shandha Rani Gope and sisters Protima Gope and Sulekha Gope (PWs 1-4) woke up and kupi light was lit and that when they opened the door the accused petitioner and coaccused Shajal Gope wanted to enter the house by force, and while Shandha Rani Gope obstructed she was assaulted by accused Jogeshwar and accused Shajal Gope also assaulted her with dagger causing bleeding injuries and accused Subash and Shajal Gope assaulted Jayanta Gope with dagger and when Susanta Gope came out of the house the accused petitioner Yogeshwar assaulted him with dagger and Susanta Gope then fell down, and on hearing the hue and cry raised by the inmates neighbours came forward. Thereafter Susanta Gope was taken to Hobigonj Sadar Hospital and was declared there dead, and then aforesaid Jayanta Gope PW | lodged First Information Report on the basis of which Hobigonj PS Case No. 29 dated 22-4-1997 was started against the petitioner and others and the police after investigation submitted charge-sheet on 29-10-1997

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: 3. The case thereupon being sent for trial, the learned Sessions Judge framed charge against the accused petitioner and others under sections 302/326/448/34 of the Penal Code to which they pleaded not guilty and claimed to be tried. During trial the prosecution examined 12 witnesses while the accused did not produce any evidence. The learned Sessions Judge after concluding the trial by the judgment and order dated 20 October 2000 convicted the accused petitioner under section 302 of the Penal Code and sentenced him to death and coaccused Subash Gope was convicted under sections 302/34 of the Penal Code and was sentenced to imprisonment for life and a fine Taka 10,000, in default, rigorous imprisonment for one year and he was also convicted under section 324 of the Penal Code and sentenced to suffer rigorous imprisonment for two years and a fine of Taka 1,000, in default, to suffer rigorous imprison ment for two months more; both the sentences to run concurrently, and co-accused Shajal Gope w acquitted 4. The learned Sessions Judge made refer ence to the High Court Division under section 334 of the Code of Criminal Procedure and the two con victs preferred Criminal Appeal No. 2901 of 2000 5. The High Court Division after hearing the parties passed the impugned judgment and order as already mentioned above

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: 6. Hence is this petition 7. In support of the petition Mr Abdul Malek learned Counsel submits, inter alia, that the High Court Division committed error in failing to consider that the prosecution failed to prove the place of occurrence and that the place of occurrence has been shifted to paddy field and not in the house of PW 1 or in his courtyard. Similarly, the manner of occurrence also has not been proved 8. He further submits that PWs 1 to 4 being father, mother and sisters of the deceased are highly interested witnesses for the prosecution and the Courts below committed illegality in convicting the petitioner based on evidence of such highly interested witnesses and that PWs 5 and 6, the neighbours, did not corroborate the evidence of PWs 1 to 4

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: 9. He strenuously submits that the story of lighting kupi bati could not be established beyond doubt and, as such, the story or recognition of the accused petitioner is not tenable and the Courts below committed illegality in not considering the said circumstances 10. We have considered the submissions made by the learned Counsel and perused the materials on record.It appears that PW 1 Jayanta Gope deposed: "তখন আমার ছেলে সুশান্ত ঘুম হইতে উঠিয়া ঘরের বাহিরে আসিলে আসামী যোগেশ্বর তাহার কোমরে থাকা ডেগার বাহির করিয়া সুশান্তের বুকের বাম পার্শ্বের নিম্নাংশে মা মারিয়া মারাত্মক জখম করে। আমার ছেলে সুশান্ত তখন চিৎকার দিয়া মাটিতে পড়িয়া যায়। আমাদের শোর চিৎকার শুনিয়া প্রতিবেশী লোকজন ঘটনাস্থলে আসেন এবং ঘটনা দেখেন ও শুনেন। সাক্ষীরা ঘটনাস্থলে আসিতে থাকিলে আসামীরা দৌড়াইয়া পলায়ন করে।"

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: 11. PW 2 Sandha Rani Gope stated: "চিৎকার শুনিয়া আমার ছেলে সুশান্ত গোপ আমার স্বামী ও আমাকে আসামীদের কবল হইতে রক্ষা করিতে আগাইয়া গেলে আসামী সজল ও সুবাস আমার ছেলে সুশান্তকে জড়াইয়া ধরে এবং আসামী যোগেশ্বর তাহার হাতে থাকা ডেগার দিয়া আমার ছেলে সুশান্তের বুকের বাম দিকে আঘাত করিয়া মারাত্মক রক্তাক্ত জখম করে। আমার ছেলে সুশান্ত তখন মাটিতে পড়িয়া যায়।" 12. PW 3 Protima Rani Gope stated:

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: "আসামী যোগেশ্বর তাহার হাতে থাকা ডেগার দিয়া আমার ভাই সুশান্তের বুকের বাম পার্শ্বে নীচে ঘাই দিয়া রক্তাক্ত জখম করে। তখন আমার ভাই সুশান্ত 'বাবা গো মারিয়া ফেলিয়াছেম্ব বলিয়া চিৎকার দিয়া মাটিতে পড়িয়া যায়। তখন সকল আসামীরা পলায়ন করে।" 13. PW 4 Sulekha Rani Gope deposed: "তখন আমার ভাই সুশান্ত শোরগোল শুনিয়া ঘুম হইতে জাগিয়া ঘর হইতে বাহির হইলে আমার মা বলে "তোর বাবাকে মারিয়া ফেলিতেছেঃ তখন আমার ভাই সুশান্ত আমার পিতাকে রক্ষা করিতে আগাইয়া গেলে আসামী সজল ও সুভাষ তাহাকে ঝাপটাইয়া ধরে এবং আসামী যোগেশ্বর তাহার হাতে থাকা ডেগার দিয়া আমার ভাই সুশান্ত গোপের বুকের বাম দিকে ডেগার দিয়া ঘাই মারিয়া মারাত্মক রক্তাক্ত জখম করে।

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: আমার ভাই সুশান্ত গোপ আঘাতপ্রাপ্ত হইয়া "বাবাগো মারিয়া ফেলিয়াছে বলিয়া চিৎকার দিয়া মাটিতে পড়িয়া যায়। আমাদের চিৎকার শুনিয়া প্রতিবেশী লোকজন ঘটনাস্থলে আসিতে থাকিলে আসামীরা পলায়ন করে।" 14. The learned Counsel however, insisting on evidence of PW 11 Amir Hossain who investigated the case initially, submits that even the officer admitted the place of occurrence sigating paddy field. In this connection, the following statement made by said witness may also be considered: "সুশান্ত খুন হওয়ার স্থানটি একটি ধান ক্ষেত সত্য নহে।". From the above deposition the submission of the learned Counsel falls through 15. The occurrence taking place in the house of PW I at about 3-00 AM it is not unlikely that the inmates of the house would become the natural witnesses. Only because of their relationship their evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: 16. We have taken into consideration the cross examination put to PWs 1 to 4 and we are of the view that their evidence remained unshaken and unblemished. From the evidence of aforesaid witnesses it is also proved that they established their claim of lighting kupi light and there is nothing to disbelieve them. In such view of the matter, we are unable to find any substance in the submissions of the learned Counsel 17. It appears that the High Court Division considering the facts and circumstances and evidence on record observed: "The death of Susanta is not denied nor it is denied Susanta died from such injury. Now, the question is, whether the prosecution could have proved its case against the condemned and convict appellants beyond all reasonable doubt that they committed the homicide. Above evidence of PW 1 Jayanta Gope of recognition is corroborated by PWs 2, 3 and 4 This PW 2 is the wife and PWs 3 and 4 daughters. At such early hour, none but those inmates of the house could be expected to witness the occurrence. PWs 2, 3 and 4 corroborating PW 1 in recognising and identifying the assailants Yogeshwar and Subhash Gope cannot be said to be improbable. It is not disputed both the appellants and the informant were closely known to each other from long before. When the son, Susanta was stabbed and fell down with scream on the ground it was not so unnatural for the father standing before him to recognise the assailants

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: Similarly, PWs 2, 3 and 4 claimed to have recognised Yogeshwar in giving the fatal blow. PW 5 Binod also stated that informant told him Yogeshwar, Shajal and Subhash killed his son. Binod Gope accompanied the informant to the hospital in taking the Susanta to the hospital. Such direct evidence in recognising Yogeshwar Gope in stabbing deceased Susanta cannot be discarded or discredited only for absence of blood at the place of occurrence. There may be various reasons for not finding blood on the place of occurrence, which was not seriously attempted to by either party to unearth

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: Similarly, failure of the prosecution to prove the motive as imputed by it will have no impact in the facts and circumstances of the case. The case of defence is that they had no enmity with the deceased Susanta. Further defence is that the informant had no idea of the occurrence and falsely implicated them only at the influence of their enemies, who accompanied the informant to the police station. PW I stated that they had enmity with the appellants. Even if such witnesses had enmity with the appellants it would be very difficult for us to accept that all PWs 1 to 5 came to the Court and on oath, told lies against the appellants In the aforesaid view of the matter we do not find any substance in the submission of Mr MA Malek. We find the learned Sessions Judge has correctly found Yogeshwar guilty under section 302 of the Penal Code for causing grievous injury which resulted in the death of Susanta." 18. We are of the view that the High Court Division passed the judgment taking a correct view

Case_Title: Yogeshwar Gope VS State
Subject_Law: Evidence Act, 1872 - Section 3
Ratio_Decidendi: Only because of relationship, witnesses' evidence cannot be thrown away unless the evidence is found to be untrue or tainted with motive.
Judgment_Content: 19. In view of the discussion made above we do not find any substance in the petition The petition is dismissed

Case_Title: Anarul Islam (Md) and others VS State and another
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Section 561A; Penal Code, 1860 - Sections 406 and 420
Ratio_Decidendi: Where the relationship between the complainant and the accused is that of Directors of a private limited company, principles of partnership shall apply. As such, spending of money by petitioner No. 1 as Managing Director cannot be termed misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company. Remedy of the complainant, if any, is by way of suit for accounts.
Judgment_Content: Md Fazlul Karim J: This appeal by leave arises out of impugned judgment and order dated 8-3-2000 passed by the High Court Division in Criminal Miscellaneous Case No. 2154 of 2000 arising out of CR Case No. 130 of 1999 corresponding to Petition Case No. 1572 of 1998 in the Court of Magistrate, 1st Class, Combined Criminal Court, 'Ka' Anchal, Gaibandha 2. The case arose out of a complaint at the instance of one Md. Hasan Karim, Director of Raqib Food Industries Private Limited at DB Road, Gaibandha on September 17, 1998 who lodged the same in the Court of Magistrate, 1st Class, Combined Criminal Court, 'Ka' Anchal, Gaibandha alleging, inter alia, that at the instigation of the accused Nos. 3 and 4 the complainant established a company namely, Raqib Food Industries Pvt Ltd at Gaibandha and the same was registered duly by the Registrar of Joint Stock Companies and the accused appellant No.1 was made Managing Director and the accused appellant No.2 was made one of the Directors of the Board of Directors: that on 15-9. 1993, a cheque amounting to Taka 42.093 was issued from the Rupali Bank at Rangpur Branch as part of the loan and the accused appellant without submitting the cheque to the account of the Raqib Food Industries deposited in his personal Account No. 921 with Rupali Bank at Gaibandha Branch vide DD 22/21 No. 578294 dated 17-9-1993 and misappropriated the same; that another cheque from Rupali Bank, Rangpur Branch dated 20-9-1993 amounting to Taka 1.25.193 in favour of the said Raqib Food Industries, the accused-appellant without depositing the same to the account of the Raqib Food Industries, deposited Taka 1,25.000 in his personal current account No. Il with Rupali Bank, Gaibandha Branch vide DD 22/26 No. 578294 dated 23-9-1993 and misappropriated the same and thus all the accused committed offence under sections 406/420/34 of the Penal Code

Case_Title: Anarul Islam (Md) and others VS State and another
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Section 561A; Penal Code, 1860 - Sections 406 and 420
Ratio_Decidendi: Where the relationship between the complainant and the accused is that of Directors of a private limited company, principles of partnership shall apply. As such, spending of money by petitioner No. 1 as Managing Director cannot be termed misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company. Remedy of the complainant, if any, is by way of suit for accounts.
Judgment_Content: 3. The accused-appellants after being informed on January 13, 2000 appeared before the Magistrate and filed application for bail and the Court below by the order dated 13-1-2000 granted bail to the accused appellants. That since the allegation of misappropriation of money belonged to the Raqib Food Industries Pvt Ltd and the accused appellant No.1 being Managing Director and the accused-appellant No.2 one of the Directors of the said Company, the complainant as one of the Directors of the said Company could not file any criminal case for misappropriating the money of the Company the appellant filed an application under section 561A of the Code of Criminal Procedure for quashing the proceeding 4. The High Court Division upon hearing the Rule held that: "From the allegations, we find clear breach of trust having been committed by the accuseds and, inasmuch as they misappropriation the money, on several occasions, as detailed in the petition of complaint and the amount also a heavy one. The breach of trust and misappro-priation have been committed by the accuseds in connivance with one another and the accuseds are very near relatives. We cannot consider the defence case sitting under section 561A of the Code of Criminal Procedure. The truth will come out in the trial after adducing of evidence by the parties in the trial Court. However, from our meticulous reading of the petition of complaint, we find prima facie cases having been established under sections 406/420 of the Penal Code. So, the accuseds should face trial and, in view of the facts and circumstances, we are not inclined to quash the proceedings."

Case_Title: Anarul Islam (Md) and others VS State and another
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Section 561A; Penal Code, 1860 - Sections 406 and 420
Ratio_Decidendi: Where the relationship between the complainant and the accused is that of Directors of a private limited company, principles of partnership shall apply. As such, spending of money by petitioner No. 1 as Managing Director cannot be termed misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company. Remedy of the complainant, if any, is by way of suit for accounts.
Judgment_Content: 5. Thereafter, the accused-appellants moved this Court in Criminal Petition for Leave to Appeal against the said impugned judgment of the High Court Division under 6. The Court granted leave to consider as "Learned Advocate for the petitioners submits that relationship between the complainant and the accused being of Directors of a Private Ltd Company principles of partner by petitioner No.1 as Managing Director of the ship shall apply and, as such, spending money company cannot be termed as misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company remedy of the complainant, if any, is by way of Civil suit for accounts and learned Judges of the High Court Division failed to appreciate that spending money by petitioner No.1 as Managing Director of the company was neither an offence under section 406 nor under section 420 of the Penal Code and, as such, erred in discharging the Rule."

Case_Title: Anarul Islam (Md) and others VS State and another
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Section 561A; Penal Code, 1860 - Sections 406 and 420
Ratio_Decidendi: Where the relationship between the complainant and the accused is that of Directors of a private limited company, principles of partnership shall apply. As such, spending of money by petitioner No. 1 as Managing Director cannot be termed misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company. Remedy of the complainant, if any, is by way of suit for accounts.
Judgment_Content: 7. Mr Mansur Habib, the learned Advocate appearing for the appellants, submitted that in view of the fact that the appellant being admittedly the Managing Director and a director of the company of which the complainants are directors and in the course of official business are alleged to have issued cheques which amount was allegedly misappropriated by the appellants, could not be legally termed as misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company for a criminal prosecution under sections 406/420 of the Penal Code and that the remedy, if there be any, is one of accounting and mesne profit for which a civil suit for accounts is the appropriate remedy and hence the prosecution of the appellant under sections 406/420 of the Penal Code is not maintainable per se and accordingly, is liable to be quashed. The learned Advocate has referred to the decisions in the case of Nasiruddin Mahmud and others vs Momtazuddin Ahmed and another reported in 1984 BLD (AD) 97 and Md Yameen and another vs KA Bashar and others reported in 1986 BLD (AD) 305 for the above proposition 8. The appeal has not been opposed, 9. In the case of Nasiruddin Mahmud and others vs. Momtazuddin Ahmed and anoiser to whether the partner can be charged by his co reported in 1984 BLD (AD) 97, the question arose s partner with an offence for the breach of trust and cheating under sections 406/420 of the Penal Code in respect of partnership business entrusting the money for the purpose, wherein it was held reviewing the decisions on the subject that a partner could not be alleged to be misappropriated cheated when an amount was entrusted to the accused for the partnership business

Case_Title: Anarul Islam (Md) and others VS State and another
Subject_Law: Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 - Section 561A; Penal Code, 1860 - Sections 406 and 420
Ratio_Decidendi: Where the relationship between the complainant and the accused is that of Directors of a private limited company, principles of partnership shall apply. As such, spending of money by petitioner No. 1 as Managing Director cannot be termed misappropriation or breach of trust of the fund of the company. Remedy of the complainant, if any, is by way of suit for accounts.
Judgment_Content: 10. Similar view has been taken in the case Ma Yameen and another vs KA Bashar and other reported in 1986 BLD (AD) 305 and the issue was as to whether the Managing Director and Chairman of a company, the trustees of the fund of the company, could be prosecuted for misappropriation of the fund of the company and as to whether the principle of law applicable to the members of the partnership shall apply to the private limited company for the proposition that the directors of the company are trustees of the money of the business in their hands and control and they could not be prosecuted for misappropriation of fund and cheating the other directors and accordingly, the prosecution against one director by another for alleged misappropriation and cheating was quashed 11. The facts and circumstances of the cited decisions apply with all force in the instant case. Accordingly, we find merit in the submissions of the learned Counsel for the appellant Accordingly, the appeal is allowed and the impugned order is set aside and the impugned party including the company may proceed against proceeding is hereby quashed. The complainan the accused-appellants for the allegations in the petition of complaint in the appropriate forum and in accordance with law

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: Md Fazlul Karim J: This appeal by leave arose out of judgment and order dated 6th May, 1992 of the High Court Division passed in Civil Revision No. 1585 of 1989 discharging the Rule issued against the judgment and order dated 7th September, 1989 by the District Judge, Hobigonj in Miscellaneous Appeal No. 66 of 1987 setting aside those dated 29th July 1987 of the Assistant Judge, Hobigonj in Miscellaneous Case No. 5 of 1986 2. The appellants instituted Miscellaneous Case for pre-emption of the case land under section 96 of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act claiming a contiguous land owner in respect of the case land transferred by respondent No.2 by kabala dated 28th October, 1979 from respondent Nos. 1 and 3 at a consideration of Taka 7,000. The case was filed within the statutory period of limitation 3. The respondent Nos. 1 and 2 jointly contested the case stating, inter alia, that the case is not maintainable for pre-emption as the transaction was not out and out sale but on mortgage with condition to purchase. The respondent No.2 took a loan of Taka 7,000 from the respondent No.1 and 3 by mortgaging the case land and in turn executed a Ekrarnama on the same date of kabala dated 28th October, 1979. The value of the land would be Taka 20,000 at the relevant period. During the pendency of the pre-emption proceeding the respondent Nos. 1 and 3 re-transferred the case land in favour of respondent No.2 by kabala dated 7th September 1981

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: 4. The learned Assistant Judge decreed the case and on appeal the same was reversed. The appellant unsuccessfully moved the High Court Division and thereafter moved this Court and obtained the leave as under: "Mr SK Sinha, learned Advocate for the pre-emptor petitioners submits that the trial Court allowed pre-emption upon a clear finding that the deed in question on the face of it is a sale deed and the alleged agreement of reconveyance is a colourable paper transaction without any consideration and that during pendency of the pre-emption proceeding the pre-emptee by a sham transaction got back the land in question in pursuance of the alleged deed of agreement of reconveyance with a view to defeating the right of pre-emption of the pre-emptors and that in arriving at the said findings the trial Court believed the evidence of PWs. and disbelieved the evidence of OPWs. upon assigning reasons therefor, but the lower appellate Court without reversing those of the trial Court and without findersing the reasoning assigned therefor illegally reversed the judgment of the trial Court and that the learned Judge of the High Court Division wrongly upheld the judgment of the lower appellate Court which is not a proper judgment of reversal in accordance with law. The learned Advocate further submits that the learned Judge of the High Court Division did not also consider the cited decision reported in 35 DLR (AD) 225 and 1994 BLD (AD) 219."

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: 5. Chowdhury Md Jahangir, the learned Advocate-on-Record appearing for the appellant, while reiterating the leave granting order, has submitted that the deed subject-matter of pre-emption being an out and out sale deed, the alleged deed for re-conveyance is a colourable transaction and that subsequent deed of transfer was a sham. document without any consideration designed to defeat the right of the pre-emption of the pre-emptor, a finding of fact as has been arrived by the trial Court on consideration of the evidence on record but the same not having been reversed by the lower appellate Court erred in law in dismissing the case and the High Court Division as well without adverting to the reasoning of the trial Court discharged the Rule resulting in error of law 6. In this respect we have gone through the evidence considered by the trial Court as under: "ওপি ডব্লিউ-১ জবানবন্দীতে বলেন যে, একরার নামার মেয়াদ ছিল ২ বৎসর। তিনি ২ বৎসর মেয়াদ অন্তে ফেরত কবলা ২/৩নং তরফছানীকে করিয়া দিয়াছেন এবং তিনি টাকা-পয়সা ফেরত দিয়াছেন।

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: ওপি ডব্লিউ-২ তিনি মোকদ্দমার ২নং তরফছানী। তিনি জবানবন্দীতে বলেন যে, ১নং তরফছানী নাঃ ভূমি ফেরত কবলা মূলে তাহার বরাবরে দেয়।তিনি জেরাতে বলেন যে, তিনি ৪নং তরফয়ানী। মাধ্যমে ফেরত কবলার পর দখল করেন। ওপি ডব্লিউ-১ জেরাতে স্বীকার করিয়াছেন খন্ তরফছানী তাহার চাচাতো ভাই। তরফতানী পক্ষ্যে দাখিলকৃত প্রঃ সঃ নং-'প কবলা হইতে দেখা যায় মামল চলাকালীন সময়ে ১নং তরফছানী নালিশার তুমি ২/৩০ তরফছানী বরাবরে বিগত ১২-৭-৮২ইং তারিজে রেজিস্ট্রিকৃত ফেরত কবলা মূলে নালিশা ভূমি ফেরত দেন? But arrived at a finding of fact that:

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: "সুতরাং প্রঃ দঃ নং 'গন্ধ দলিল যে অগ্রক্রয়ের দাবীতে প্রতিহত করার জন্য মূল্য শূন্য ও কাগজ করাবার আরা সুই করা হইয়াছে তাহা বলার বা প্রমাণের অপেক্ষা রাখে ন প্রঃ দঃ নং 'গম্ব দলিল মূল্য শূন্য ও কাগজ কাজ করাবাজ এবং উক্ত দলিল দ্বারা ২/৩নং দরফছানী নালিশা ভূমিয়ে দখলে যান নাই এবং তাহা ১নং তরফছানী দখলে আঙ বলিয়া মনে করি।” 7. On perusal of the judgment of the Coun of appeal below it appears that the learned Distno Judge has considered, inter alia, that: "তরফছানী পক্ষের ৩নং সাক্ষ্য রামলাল সরকার তাহার জবানবন্দীতে বলিয়াছেন যে, তিনি ২৮-১০-৭৯৯ তারিখের কবলা ও একরারনামাও ৭-৯-৮১ইং তারিখে। ফেরত কবলার লেখক। তিনি বলিয়াছেন যে, উক্ত কবলা। টাকা পয়সা লেনদেন হয় এবং কবলা সত্য। তরফছানী পক্ষের ৪নং সাক্ষী আলকাছ মিয়া ওরফে কাচা মিয় একরারনামা প্রদর্শনী খ এর সাক্ষী। তিনি তাহার জবানবন্দীতে বলিয়াছেন যে, ১নং তরফছানী ২ (দুই) বৎসা নালিশী ভূমিতে দখলকার ছিলেন এবং ২(দুই) বৎসর মেয়াদের মধ্যে ফেরত কবলা মূলে ২নং তরফছানী বরাবর ফেরত কবলা করিয়া দখল সমজাইয়া দেন। কাজেই দেখ যায় যে, ২/৩নং তরফছানীগণ ১নং তরফছানীর নিকট নালিশা ভূমি ৭,০০০ টাকা মূল্যে বিক্রয় করিয়া ছিলেন এবং একই তারিখে ১নং তরফছানী ২/৩নং তরফছানী বরাবরে একখানা নামা সম্পাদন করিয়া অঙ্গিকার করেন যে ২ বৎসরের মধ্যে মূল্যের টাকা ফেরত দিলে নালিশা ভূটি ফেরত দিবেন এবং উক্ত একরারনামার শর্তানুসারে ১নং তরফছানী ২/৩নং তরফছানীর নিকট হইতে মূল্যের টাক বুঝিয়া পাইয়া ৭-৯-৮১ইং তারিখে রেজিস্ট্রি কবলা মুলে নালিশা ভূমি ২/৩নং তরফছানী বরাবরে ফেরত দেন।"

Judgment_Content: 8. From the above, it cannot be alleged that the Court of appeal below has not reversed the finding that the deed in question is sale deed and that the alleged deed of agreement for reconveyance are colourable paper transactions without any consideration as the Court of appeal below on consideration of the material on record, including evidence arrived at a finding of fact that after the transaction the mortgagee has delivered the possession of the case land to the mortgagor pursuant to deed of reconveyance 9. It may be mentioned here that the evidence on record do not support the finding of fact arrived at by the trial Court and the type and nature of discussion, consideration and finding arrived at as quoted above could not be treated as a finding of fact of the trial Court because the same did not manifest to be a conclusive one arrived at by the arial Court 10. As regards the learned Advocate-on-Record next submitted that the High Court Division did not consider the cited decisions applicable in the facts of the present case reported in 35 DLR (AD) 225, 1994 BLD (AD) 219

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: IL. In the case of Shafi Khan vs Mannujan Hossain reported in 35 DLR (AD) 225 the principles laid down therein is that whether the right of pre-emption is available when the land sought to be pre-empted has already been reconveyed to the vendor, particularly when the deed of reconveyance has been executed and registered before the filing of the application for pre-emption. The short fact of the case is that the land under pre-emption was sold by one Abdul Bari on 28th March, 1974 to his nephew Ayesh Khan by a registered sale deed dated 31st October, 1974. A co-sharer of Abdul Bari filed an application on 1st November, 1974 for pre-emption under section 24 of the Non-Agricultural Tenancy Act alleging that the transfer to Ayesh Khan had become absolute as soon as the kabala was registered and that the subsequent reconveyance was fraudulent and collusive intended to defeat his statutory right of pre-emption 12. This court considered two sets of decision as under:

Case_Title: Yaor Mia (Md) and another VS Haji Shah Dhanai Ali and others
Subject_Law: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950 - Sections 95(4), 95(5) and 95A; President's Order No. 88 of 1972
Ratio_Decidendi: After enjoyment of usufructs for a maximum period of 7 years under section 95A of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, the right to get back the property accrues. In that event the provision of the Transfer of Property Act shall not apply to such kind of transaction except those which were not alive when President's Order No. 88 of 1972 was promulgated, being transactions past and closed.
Judgment_Content: "In Abdus Sattar Mallick vs Younus Mallick and ors, 12 DLR 849 the question of pre-emption under section 26F, Bengal Tenancy Act, came up for consideration before Hamoodur Rahman, J (as his Lordship then was) and, on examination of a number of cases it was held that operation of section 26F could not be jeopardised by an agreement for reconveyance and that the right to pre-emption being a statutory right, it will prevail over any agreement for reconveyance. It was further held that as soon as "the holding is transferred by a sale, the sale becomes absolute and the condition for reconveyance cannot suspend operation of a statute" and that by a sale the interest of the property passed and "what remained was merely a right to obtain a reconveyance, or, in other words, a right under a contract for sale. The latter right could not be treated as a right in the property itself and hence, the pre-emption could not be resisted". In that case a reference was made to the cases of Tarapada Karati vs Sudhamoy Doulbi and others 53 CWN 678 and Nishi Kanta Das vs Jnanendra Nath Mondal and others 5% CWN 253. In those cases a similar view was taken by Chakraborty, J. But in the case of Biswaswar Roy vs Joytennessa and others 13 DLR 287. Sattar J held that the right of pre-emption is not absolute and is subject to any agreement for reconveyance and if the right of pre-emption is enforced with notice of the agreement for reconveyance the pre-emptor will take the property subject to the agreement for reconveyance. It has been further held that "if a reconveyance has been taken already and the land has gone back to the original owner there is no question of enforcement of the right of pre-emption". The learned Judge has taken note of the contrary view expressed by Rahman, J in the case of Abdus Sattar Mallik vs Younus Mallik and others 12 DLR 849. As to the views taken by Chakraborty, I in the case of Nishi Kanta Das vs Jnanendra Nath Mondal, 57 CWN 253 confirming his earlier view taken in the case of Tarapada Karati vs Sudhamoy, 53 CWN 678, as already mentioned above, Sattar J…

Case_Title: Bangladesh represented by the Secretary, Ministry of Establishment and others VS Abdul Halim Miah and others
Subject_Law: Supreme Court of Bangladesh (Appellate Division) Rules, 1988 - Order XXIV; Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Order XLVII, Rule 1
Ratio_Decidendi: Review under Order XXIV of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh (Appellate Division) Rules, 1988 or under Order XLVII, rule 1 of the Code of Civil Procedure is subject to law and rules but cannot be a substitute for an appeal for re-hearing on the impugned judgment and order passed in appeal and for correcting the same unless there is an error apparent on the face of the record based on evidence or material on record.
Judgment_Content: Md Fazlul Karim J: Respondent Nos. 1-10 2002 seek review of the judgment and order dat the Civil Appeal being Nos. 69, 70, 71 and 22 4th of May 2003 passed by this Division allows the appeals upon setting aside the judgment (Civil Appeal Nos. 69 and 71 of 2002) heand Court Division in Writ Petition No. 62 of 200 order dated 17th of December 2000 of the Hi Writ Petition No. 5362 of 2000 (Civil appeal nos 70 and 72 of 2002). The said appeals arose out of Court Division making the Rules absolute in Writ Pelion Nos, 62 of 2000 and 5362 of 2000 declaring the impugned order in Memo dated 27-9-1998 Annexure-K issued by the respondent No. I and the impugned decisions under item Nos. 12 and 13 as adopted/taken at the meeting of the Standing and 6 on 2-6-1999 and 20-9-1999 contained in the, minutes (Annexure-'M') and of the gradation list issued and published by respondent Nos. 2 and 8 in the Bangladesh Gazette on 23-11-1999 placing writ-respondent Nos. 11-79 at serial Nos. 115 to 183 therein (Annexure-'N') and the remarks under clause 9 of the gradation list against the names of the writ petitioner-respondents unconstitutional, void and without lawful authority and further directing the writ respondent Nos. 1-10 to follow the final gradation list published on 1-10-1990 (Annexure-H) relating to the writ respondents and other writ respondents

Case_Title: Bangladesh represented by the Secretary, Ministry of Establishment and others VS Abdul Halim Miah and others
Subject_Law: Supreme Court of Bangladesh (Appellate Division) Rules, 1988 - Order XXIV; Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Order XLVII, Rule 1
Ratio_Decidendi: Review under Order XXIV of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh (Appellate Division) Rules, 1988 or under Order XLVII, rule 1 of the Code of Civil Procedure is subject to law and rules but cannot be a substitute for an appeal for re-hearing on the impugned judgment and order passed in appeal and for correcting the same unless there is an error apparent on the face of the record based on evidence or material on record.
Judgment_Content: 2. The facts relevant for disposal of these appeals are common as detailed in those writ petitions, inter alia, that the writ-petitioner Nos. 1 and 2 and writ petitioner Nos. 3-10 of Writ Petition No. 62 of 2000 were appointed on 30-9-76 and on 17-12-76 respectively and the writ petitioner Md. Abdus Salam of Writ Petition No. 5362 of 2000 was appointed on 17-12-76 as the Assistant Engineer (Civil) of Roads and Highways. All these appointments were on ad-hoc basis for six months or till regularisation of service by the Public Service Commission briefly, the PSC. This period was extended from time to time. The PSC published an advertisement on 30-3-1978 and in that advertisement it was mentioned that the Assistant Engineers of Roads and Highways Department who were appointed on ad-hoc basis. might apply for regularisation of their service. The writ petitioner No. 1 Md Halim Miah of Writ Petition No. 62 of 2000 and the writ petitioner Md Abdus Salam of Writ Petition No. 5362 of 2000 applied for regularisation of their service. Subsequent advertisement of similar nature was made by the PSC on 1-4-78 and the writ petitioners Nos. 2-10 of writ petition No. 62 of 2000 applied for regularisation of their service. All the wit interview before the Public Service Commission on those dates and came out successful. Because of their success at their first appearance before the commission, the service of all writ petitioners were regularised on 9-3-1979. The Government, on the recommendation of the Council Committee and in seniority of ad-hoc appointees, after regularisation, would be counted from the date of their ad hoc appointment. The ad-hoc appointments of the writ petitioners and others were quite different from the ad-hoc appointments made before 1972 and regular scales of pay, yearly increments and efficiency bar crossing were allowed to them from the very beginning and the period of appointment was extended far beyond six months and thereby clearly indicating that the appointments of the writ petitioners were in fact quasi-permanent. The memo of the Establishment Division dated 23-8-73 in its dated 22-4-1984 (Annexure-K-2) providing that paragraph 3 provided that besides the appointment mentioned in Regulations 39 and 40 of the PSC the Government decided that all appointments under the Government of Bangladesh shall be made on ad-hoc basis till decision of the Government on the report of the Administrative and Service Re-organisation Committee and the repatriation of Bangladeshi employees stranded in Pakistan. The paragraph 4 of the aforesaid memo further provides for regular yearly increments

Judgment_Content: 5. According to the said Rule 4(1), the ad-hoc appointees who were appointed on ad-hoc basis from 9-4-72 to 12-5-83 and were regularised during this period, would count their seniority from the dates of their respective appointment on ad-hoc basis. The ad-hoc appointees appointed between 9. 4-72 to 12-5-83 and regularized in service within this period would count their seniority from the date of their ad-hoc appointment with the exception that the ad-hoc appointees who failed to get recommendation by the PSC for regularisation in the first time shall not be entitled to claim senionty from the date of the ad-hoc appointment as provided in rule 4(2)(Gha). The seniority of the writ petitioners was thus counted from the date of their ad-hoc appointments from 30-9-76 and 17-12-76 respectively. Because of the writ petitioner's success at the first appearance in the interview before the Public Service Commission between the period from 9-4-72 and 12-5-83 they are similarly situated and all of them are entitled to have their seniority counted from the date of their ad-hoc appointment and no discrimination can be made with others & The Ministry of Establishment vide its memo dated 30-12-90 (Annexure-B) gave its interpretation to Rule 4(2)(Gha) of Ad-hoc Service Counting Rules, 1990 which runs thus "যারাঃ (২)(ঘ) এ বলা আছে এডহক তারিখ হইতে জ্যেষ্ঠতা গণনা করার সুবিধা তাহারাই পাইবেন না যাহারা অন্যান্য শর্তাবলীর মধ্যে নিয়মিতভাবে নিযুক্তির জন্য তমিশন কর্তৃক পরিচালিত পরীক্ষা বা সাক্ষাৎকারে প্রথম তারে ইহার সুপারিশলাভে ব্যর্থ হইয়াছেন এমন কর্মকর্তা। এই প্রথম বার বলিতে নিয়মিতভাবে নিযুক্তির জন্য কমিশন কর্তৃক পরিচালিত পরীক্ষার/সাক্ষাৎকারের মাধ্যমে সংশ্লিষ্ট প্রার্থী যে পরীক্ষায় বা সাক্ষাৎকারে প্রথম উপস্থিত হইয়াছিলেন, সেইটাই তাহার জন্য প্রথম পরীক্ষার/সাক্ষাৎকার হিসাবে গণ্য হইবে এবং উক্ত পরীক্ষায়/সাক্ষাৎকারে কৃতকার্য হইলেই তিনি এডহক নিয়োগের তারিখ হইতে জ্যেষ্ঠতা পাইবেন।

The Ministry of Establishment, contrary to the decision of the Appellate Division in Civil Appeal Nos. 44, 45, 46, 47, 48, 49, 62, 63, 76 and 77 of 1992 and of the High Court Division in Writ Petition No. 316 of 1995 and in disregard of the interpretation given by it on 30-12-1990 reiterated the same as aforesaid on 18-9-94, by the impugned order of interpretation dated 27-9-98, the vested rights of seniority of the writ petitioners recognised in the gradation list published in Bangladesh Gazette on 1-10-1990 and all other rights enjoyed by them from the date of ad-hoc appointment since 30-9-1976 have been affected

In the reported case of Bangladesh vs Azizur Rahman this Division found the classifi-cation made in Rule 4(1)(Ka) to be lawful and constitutional but held that the classification made in Rule 4(1)(kha) to be discriminatory and, as such violative of Articles 27 and 29 of the Constitution, Samilarly, rule 5(2) giving seniority to ad-hoc appointment and not from the regular appointment adversely affecting writ petitioner's right to equal the Rules the ad-hoc appointees were divided into opportunity was declared unconstitutional. Under two classes, one under Rule 4(1)(Ka) i.e., those who were appointed at any time before 9th April, 1972 AD 14 on ad-hoc basis but were regularised at any time by 12th May, 1983 and the others under rule 4(1)(Kha) ie those who were appointed an ad-hoc basis from 9th April, 1972 to 12th May 1983 and were regularised during this period. Rule 5(1) provided that the officers of the first category would count their seniority from 9th April, 1972 and rule 5(2) provided that the officers in the 2nd category would respective appointments on ad-hoc basis. The count their seniority from the dates of their Appellate Division while allowing the appeal in the aforesaid decision found that: "The main ground of the classification is to do justice to a larger number of ad-hoc employees appointed during this period as a matter of policy as well as of necessity, by giving benefit of continuous ad-hoc service and this benefit has been equally given to both the writ petitioners and the respondents. So, the doctrine of severeability is not applicable to their case as it is same to others."

Judgment_Content: "Lastly, Mr Ishtiaq Ahmed has cited a few cases in which some of the ad-hoc appointees, who did not appear before the PSC at all but were regularised under the Regularisation Rules of 1983, have been granted seniority from the date of their ad-hoc appointments. This, the learned Counsel contends, contravenes Rule 4(2)(gha) of the impugned Rules. Rule 4(2)(gha) provides that those ad-hoc appointees who failed to get a recommendation from the PSC "at the first opportunity" stand excluded from the benefit of seniority from the date of ad-hoc appointments. The learned Counsel argues that those who did not appear before the PSC come under this category that is, those who failed to get a recommendation of the PSC. But the learned Additional Attorney-General wants to restrict sub-clause (Gha) only to those ad-hoc appointees who appeared before the PSC but failed to get a recommendation at the first chance and that those who could not appear before the PSC due to circumstances beyond their control were intended to be excluded from the purview of sub-clause (Gha). Mr TH Khan argues that the interpretation of this sub-clause (Gha) has been left undecided by the High Court Division and, as such, this need not be interpreted in these appeals, particularly when its interpretation depends upon factual determination of certain question. He has also argued that a case in respect of this very question is still pending before the Administrative Tribunal. In all these circumstances I would leave this question open."

Ratio_Decidendi: Review under Order XXIV of the Supreme Court of Bangladesh (Appellate Division) Rules, 1988 or under Order XLVII, rule 1 of the Code of Civil Procedure is subject to law and rules but cannot be a substitute for an appeal for re-hearing on the impugned judgment and order passed in appeal and for correcting the same unless there is an error apparent on the face of the record based on evidence or material on record.
Judgment_Content: "In view of the aforesaid findings, the grounds upon which review of the judgment is sought have already been decided. A wrong decision on interpretation of certain provision of law or principle laid down in a decision relied upon by a Court are no grounds for review "Error apparent on the face of the record" cannot be said to be so where a lengthy argument is advanced to explain such Therefore, on the grounds urged for review of apressions as is argued by the petitioner. judgment by the petitioner, it seems to us that we are asked to sit over our judgment by way of appeal in a circuitous way addressing lengthy and repeated submissions which already received our due notice in the judgment sought to be reviewed." These petitions are, accordingly, dismissed
